Amazon Sacred Headwaters Alliance

network_affiliation
[[BioFi Project]]
type
organization
location_lat
-1.5
background_section
## Background Where the Andes' glacial peaks descend to meet the Amazon basin, an experiment in intercultural governance is taking shape across [35 million hectares](https://cuencasagradas.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/10/ENG_PLAN-BIOREGIONAL_10_2021.pdf) of one of Earth's most biodiverse regions. The bioregion encompasses more than [50 distinct ecosystems](https://youtu.be/4VG88nc2s6E), fed by rich hydrology flowing from Andean glaciers into the Amazon. The region plays a crucial role in carbon absorption, with 60% of the bioregion composed of [protected areas and Indigenous territories](https://youtu.be/4VG88nc2s6E), creating a unique governance landscape where conservation and indigenous sovereignty intersect. Across these territories spanning the Ecuador-Peru border, [more than 30 indigenous peoples and nationalities](https://youtu.be/4VG88nc2s6E) maintain their own territories and languages: Andas, Achuaras, Kofanes, Secoyas, Shuar, Kukama, Iquito, Siona, Kichwa, and others. While each community faces distinct challenges, they share systemic pressures from extractive industries, infrastructure development, and environmental degradation. ### Origins and Network The Amazon Sacred Headwaters Alliance was born in 2017 when indigenous nations from Ecuador and Peru issued an urgent call to protect this megadiverse bioregion. Their vision: develop a bioregional plan that articulates realistic solutions to address the economic and social challenges facing the region while protecting its ecological and cultural integrity. The initiative maps how indigenous territories connect across national borders, creating what participants describe as a "mosaic of interculturality and plurinationality." This vision aligns with Ecuador's constitutional recognition of interculturality and plurinationality as foundational principles of the state. However, the alliance faces a fundamental challenge: Peru's legal framework differs significantly from Ecuador's, creating governance complexities when states fail to recognize indigenous rights equally.

temp
issues with location
description
Established in 2017, the Amazon Sacred Headwaters Alliance unites indigenous nationalities across Ecuador and Peru to protect their ancestral territories. The alliance operates through indigenous-led governance, cross-border collaboration, integration of traditional decision-making, and strategic partnerships between indigenous communities and NGOs to safeguard one of the Amazon's most biodiverse regions.
id
amazon-sacred-headwaters-alliance
vision_legacy_section
## Vision and Legacy The vision articulated toward 2030 envisions this bioregion permanently protected and restored as a living bioregion, inspired by indigenous people's stewardship vision and practices. The plan envisions people and forests flourishing with a prosperous and inclusive well-being economy. The region would contribute to reversing global warming and biodiversity loss, serving as an inspiring model for a just ecological transition. The comprehensive approach to ecosocial transition represents a significant effort to balance environmental conservation with social development, while centering indigenous rights and knowledge systems. Its success depends on sustained political will, adequate funding, and effective implementation of the proposed mechanisms, all while maintaining the cultural and ecological integrity of the Sacred Headwaters region.
website
https://sacredheadwaters.org/
bioregional_context
The region encompasses 35 million hectares spanning Ecuador and Peru, where the Andes mountain range meets the Amazon basin. This creates dramatic elevation changes from 6,000 meters in the Andean glaciers to less than 500 meters in the Amazon lowlands. The area represents one of the Amazon's most biodiverse hotspots, containing approximately 50 distinct ecosystems and numerous indigenous territories.
category
Bioregional Organizer
